### Example

Passage:
(1) It's a warm summer afternoon.
(2) Suddenly, a few raindrops fall on your arms, and before you know it, the sky is opened up.
(3) Heading to your car is almost always the safest choice.
(4) Your tent and a picnic shelter will keep you dry, but they offer little or no protection against lightning.
(5) Your car, on the other hand, will keep you dry and protect you from lightning strikes.
(6) Protecting yourself against possible lightning strikes is very important; lightning kills an average of 62 people in the US each year.
(7) If you keep all of the windows and doors closed in your car, you'll be able to return to your campsite safely.
(8) What else should you do?

Statement: Lightning is a danger for campers.

Step-by-step reasoning:

The passage discusses what to do when it suddenly starts raining during a summer afternoon. It emphasizes that a car is the safest place to be during a storm, especially compared to tents or picnic shelters. It explicitly says that tents and shelters "offer little or no protection against lightning." It also notes that "lightning kills an average of 62 people in the US each year," highlighting its danger. Since camping usually involves tents and outdoor shelters, and the passage warns that these do not protect well from lightning, it implies that lightning is a danger specifically for people in those situations — including campers. Therefore, the statement "Lightning is a danger for campers" is: True

Answer: True

Then, at the end, answer with either "True" if the statement is supported, or "Not True" if it is not supported or contradicted.

Think step-by-step.
After your reasoning, return your answer in the following format:

```
Answer: Not True
```

---

### Example

Passage:
(1) It's a warm summer afternoon.
(2) Suddenly, a few raindrops fall on your arms, and before you know it, the sky is opened up.
(3) Heading to your car is almost always the safest choice.
(4) Your tent and a picnic shelter will keep you dry, but they offer little or no protection against lightning.
(5) Your car, on the other hand, will keep you dry and protect you from lightning strikes.
(6) Protecting yourself against possible lightning strikes is very important; lightning kills an average of 62 people in the US each year.
(7) If you keep all of the windows and doors closed in your car, you'll be able to return to your campsite safely.
(8) What else should you do?

Statement: Lightning is a danger for campers.

Step-by-step reasoning:

The passage discusses what to do when it suddenly starts raining during a summer afternoon. It emphasizes that a car is the safest place to be during a storm, especially compared to tents or picnic shelters. It explicitly says that tents and shelters "offer little or no protection against lightning." It also notes that "lightning kills an average of 62 people in the US each year," highlighting its danger. Since camping usually involves tents and outdoor shelters, and the passage warns that these do not protect well from lightning, it implies that lightning is a danger specifically for people in those situations — including campers. Therefore, the statement "Lightning is a danger for campers" is: True

Answer: True

(1) I was now in my twenty third year of residence in this island and was accustomed to the place and to the manner of living.
(2) If it had not been the savages who had come to the place to disturb me, I could have been content to spent the rest of my time there, even to the last moment, till I had laid me down and died, like the old goat in the cave.
(3) I had also arrived to some little recreations and amusements, which made the time pass more pleasantly with me a great deal than it did before.
(4) At first, I had taught my Poll to speak.
(5) And he did it so familiarly and talked so clearly and plain that it was very pleasant to me.
(6) And he lived with me no less than twenty six years.
(7) How long he might live afterwards, I don't know; though I know they have an idea in Brazil that they live a hundred years.
(8) Perhaps poor Poll may be alive there still, calling Poor Robin Crusoe to this day.
(9) I wish no other English man had the bad luck to go there and hear him.
(10) But if he did, he would certainly believe it was the devil .
(11) My dog was a very pleasant and loving companion to me, for no less than sixteen years of my time, and then died of mere old age.
(12) As for my cats, they multiplied to that degree that I had to shoot several of them at first to keep them from eating up all I had.
(13) Besides these, I had two more parrots which talked pretty well and would all call Robin Crusoe, but none like my first.
(14) Nor indeed did I take the pains with any of them that I had done with him.
(15) I had also several sea-fowls, whose names I don't know, that I caught upon the shore and cut their wings.
(16) And the little stakes which I had planted before my castle wall being now grown up to a good thick bush, these fowls all lived among these low trees and bred there, which was very agreeable to me; so that as I said above, I began to be well content with the life I led if not worried about the threat from the savages.

(3) I had also arrived to some little recreations and amusements, which made the time pass more pleasantly with me a great deal than it did before.
(4) At first, I had taught my Poll to speak.
(5) And he did it so familiarly and talked so clearly and plain that it was very pleasant to me.
(6) And he lived with me no less than twenty six years.
(7) How long he might live afterwards, I don't know; though I know they have an idea in Brazil that they live a hundred years.
(8) Perhaps poor Poll may be alive there still, calling Poor Robin Crusoe to this day.
(9) I wish no other English man had the bad luck to go there and hear him.
(10) But if he did, he would certainly believe it was the devil .
(11) My dog was a very pleasant and loving companion to me, for no less than sixteen years of my time, and then died of mere old age.

(15) I had also several sea-fowls, whose names I don't know, that I caught upon the shore and cut their wings.

responses:

4. The passage does not provide any information about interactions or relationships with the savages, other than indicating that their presence was a disturbance.

5. The statement "Robin Crusoe didn't live in harmony with savages" is supported by the passage because it explicitly mentions that the savages disturbed the person, implying a lack of harmony.

Answer: True

(8) Over time, bone is said to be lost because it's not being replaced.

a|true|high20111.txt.passage:
(1) Where is that noise coming from?
(2) Not sure?
(3) Try living with your eyes closed for a few years.
(4) Blind people are better at locating sounds than people who can see, a new study says, without the benefits of vision the ears seem to work much better.
(5) Previous studies have shown that blind people are better than others at reaching out and touching the sources of sounds that are close by.
(6) Researchers from the University of Montreal wanted to see if blind people were also better at locating sounds that are far away.
(7) Twenty-three blind people participated in the study.
(8) All had been sightless for at least 20 years.
(9) Fourteen of them had lost their vision before age 11. the rest went blind after age 16.
(10) The experiment also included 10 people who could see but were wearing blind-folds.
(11) In one task, volunteers had to pick the direction of a sound coming from about 3 metres away.
(12) When the sound was in front of them or slightly off center in front, both groups performed equally well.
(13) When sounds came from the side or the back, however, the blind group performed much better than the blindfolded group.
(14) The participants who had been blind since childhood did slightly better than those who lost their sight later.
(15) Recognizing the locations of distant sounds can be a matter of life-or-death for blind people, say the researchers.
(16) Crossing the street, for instance, is much harder when you can't see the cars coming.
(17) Still, the researchers were surprised by how well the blind participants did, especially those who went blind after age 16.
(18) In another experiment, the scientists also found that parts of the brain that normally deal with visual information became active in locating sound in the people who were blind by age 11.
(19) These brain parts didn't show sound-location activity in the other group of blind people or in the sighted people.
(20) The scientists now want to learn more about the working of brains of "late-onset" blind people.

(1) Hundreds of students from around the world gathered in New York City last week for the Microsoft Imagine Cup finals.
(2) They came to present their ideas for using technology to solve world problems.
(3) Microsoft education director Suzi Levine says the nine-year-old program began mainly as a competition to create technology.
(4) SUZI LEVINE: "When we realized that students really actually want to have a purpose for what they're creating, we introduced the idea of inspiring them with the UN Millennium Development Goals and suggesting that they use those for their muse .
(5) "This past year we also rolled out something called the Imagine Cup Solve This library , where IGOs, NGOs and nonprofits can submit some of the technical challenges that they would like students to consider for their solutions."
(6) Microsoft says over 350,000 high school and college students registered for this year's competition.
(7) Judges chose more than four hundred of them to attend the finals.
(8) SUZI LEVINE: "One from Thailand was called NewKrean, where they created a Windows Phone 7 application that allows you to broadcast your location to your social network of friends so that you can be more easily rescued."
(9) They named their application Terra.
(10) Suzi Levine says there were also ideas from Egypt inspired by the revolution that removed president Hosni Mubarak in February.
(11) SUZI LEVINE: "One was to use Bluetooth as sort of a Twitter equivalent so that if the government shuts down the Internet, you actually can still have a massive social distribution."
(12) Students competed in nine categories.
(13) For example, in software design the top prize of twenty-five thousand dollars went to Team Hermes from Ireland.
(14) The students developed a device for cars to collect information on road conditions, driving behavior and traffic incidents.
(15) A team from Taiwan's National Tsing Hua University won first place in the embedded  development category.
(16) They developed a network of wireless devices to help plot the safest escape routes during a fire.
(17) Next year's awards ceremony will take place in Australia.
(18) Registration for Imagine Cup twenty-twelve opened Friday.
(19) Also, Microsoft announced plans for a three million dollar program to help Imagine Cup winners further develop their projects.

(1) If you like bicycling as much as you like playing ball and can't seem to decide between the two sports, you may want to consider cycle ball--a fun sport that adopts the key attributes from both activities and transforms it into a totally new game.
(2) Also known as red ball, the game was invented in 1893 by German laborers who wished to play polo , but could not afford horses.
(3) While the sport has not caught on in North America, it is very fashionable in Europe and even Japan.
(4) Played in an indoor court, cycle ball is similar to soccer in many ways--the aim is to score as many goals as possible, and only the goalkeeper is allowed to touch the ball.
(5) The one big difference?

(6) Held on a farm, the Glastonbury Festival is the most well-known and popular festival in the UK.
(7) It began in 1970 and the first festival was attended by one thousand five hundred people each paying an admission price of PS1 -- the ticket included free milk from the farm.
(8) Since then the Glastonbury Festival has gone from strength to strength -- in 2004 one hundred and fifty thousand fans attended, paying PS112 each for a ticket to the three-day event.
(9) Tickets for the event sold out within three hours.
(10) Performers included superstars, such as Paul McCartney and James Brown, as well as new talent, like Franz Ferdinand and Joss Stone.
(11) Although many summer festivals are run on a profit-making basis, Glastonbury is a charity event, donating millions of pounds to local and international charities.
(12) Glastonbury is not unique  in using live music to raise money to fight global poverty.
(13) In July of this year, the Live 8 concerts were held simultaneously   in London, Paris, Rome and Berlin.
(14) Superstars such as Madonna, Sir Elton John and Stevie Wonder performed in order to highlight international poverty and debt.

You are given a passage and a statement. Your task is to determine whether the statement is supported by the passage. 

First, explain your reasoning step by step based only on the information in the passage. 
Then, at the end, answer with either "True" if the statement is supported, or "Not True" if it is not supported or contradicted.

Think step-by-step.
After your reasoning, return your answer in the following format:

```
Answer: Not True
```

---

### Example

Passage:
(1) It's a warm summer afternoon.
(2) Suddenly, a few raindrops fall on your arms, and before you know it, the sky is opened up.
(3) Heading to your car is almost always the safest choice.
(4) Your tent and a picnic shelter will keep you dry, but they offer little or no protection against lightning.
(5) Your car, on the other hand, will keep you dry and protect you from lightning strikes.
(6) Protecting yourself against possible lightning strikes is very important; lightning kills an average of 62 people in the US each year.
(7) If you keep all of the windows and doors closed in your car, you'll be able to return to your campsite safely.
(8) What else should you do?

Statement: Lightning is a danger for campers.

Step-by-step reasoning:

The passage discusses what to do when it suddenly starts raining during a summer afternoon. It emphasizes that a car is the safest place to be during a storm, especially compared to tents or picnic shelters. It explicitly says that tents and shelters "offer little or no protection against lightning." It also notes that "lightning kills an average of 62 people in the US each year," highlighting its danger. Since camping usually involves tents and outdoor shelters, and the passage warns that these do not protect well from lightning, it implies that lightning is a danger specifically for people in those situations — including campers. Therefore, the statement "Lightning is a danger for campers" is: True

Answer: True

2. **Submission Process**: According to sentence (13), to upload a job advertisement, one must log in to the Careers Centre employer database, fill out a form, agree to the advertising terms and conditions, and submit the advertisement for review by the Careers Centre.

5. **Conditions for Display**: The passage does not explicitly state that all submitted ads will not appear online. Instead, it implies that ads are reviewed and, if approved, will be displayed.

6. **Conclusion**: The statement "All job ads will not appear online once they are submitted" suggests that no ads appear online after submission, which contradicts the passage's implication that ads are reviewed and can be displayed if they meet the criteria.

(4) Your tent and a picnic shelter will keep you dry, but they offer little or no protection against lightning.
(5) Your car, on the other hand, will keep you dry and protect you from lightning strikes.
(6) Protecting yourself against possible lightning strikes is very important; lightning kills an average of 62 people in the US each year.
(7) If you keep all of the windows and doors closed in your car, you'll be able to return to your campsite safely.
(8) What else should you do?

Statement: Lightning is a danger for campers.

It emphasizes that a car is the safest place to be during a storm, especially compared to tents or picnic shelters. It explicitly says that tents and shelters "offer little or no protection against lightning." It also notes that "lightning kills an average of 62 people in the US each year," highlighting its danger. Since camping usually involves tents and outdoor shelters, and the passage warns that these do not protect well from lightning, it implies that lightning is a danger specifically for people in those situations — including campers. Therefore, the statement "Lightning is a danger for campers" is: True

a|true|high22007.txt.passage:
(1) About 10 years ago, I started a job as a trainer for a telemarketing agency.
(2) In one of my first classes, the head trainer told a very inspiring story.
(3) He began by drawing a stickman standing in the middle of a circle.
(4) To make it more interesting, he drew things like a house, a car, and a few friends inside the circle.
(5) He asked the question, "Can anyone tell me what this is?"
(6) In a long silence, one guy decided to throw out "the world?"
(7) The trainer said, "That's close and this is your Comfort Zone.
(8) Inside your circle you have all the things that are important to you. "
(9) "Your home, your family, your friends, and your job.
(10) People feel that inside this circle they are safe from any danger or conflict. "
(11) "Can anyone tell me what happens when you step out of this circle?"
(12) A strong silence came over the room.
(13) The same eager guy abruptly announced, "You are afraid."
(14) Another guy said, "You make mistakes."
(15) The silence continued and the trainer smiled and said, "When you make mistakes, what can the result be?"
(16) The first guy shouted, "You learn something."
(17) "Exactly, you are learning."
(18) The trainer turned to the board and drew an arrow pointing from the stickman directly to the outside of the circle.
(19) He continued to say, "When you leave your Comfort Zone you put yourself out there, in front of the world to be in situations that you are not comfortable with."
(20) "The end result is that you have learned something that you did not know and you expand your knowledge to become a better person."
(21) He turned again to the board and drew a bigger circle around the original circle, and added a few new things like more friends, a bigger house, etc.
(22) "The moral of the story is that if you stay inside your Comfort Zone, you will never be able to expand your horizons and learn.
(23) When you step out of your Comfort Zone you will eventually make your circle bigger and your mind grow stronger, and all in all a better person."
a|true|high22007.txt.statement: If we move beyond the Comfort Zone, our anxiety level will go up.

First, explain your reasoning step by step based only on the information in the passage. 
Then, at the end, answer with either "True" if the statement is supported, or "Not True" if it is not supported or contradicted.

Think step-by-step.
After your reasoning, return your answer in the following format:

```
Answer: Not True
```

---

### Example

Passage:
(1) It's a warm summer afternoon.
(2) Suddenly, a few raindrops fall on your arms, and before you know it, the sky is opened up.
(3) Heading to your car is almost always the safest choice.
(4) Your tent and a picnic shelter will keep you dry, but they offer little or no protection against lightning.
(5) Your car, on the other hand, will keep you dry and protect you from lightning strikes.
(6) Protecting yourself against possible lightning strikes is very important; lightning kills an average of 62 people in the US each year.
(7) If you keep all of the windows and doors closed in your car, you'll be able to return to your campsite safely.
(8) What else should you do?

Statement: Lightning is a danger for campers.

Step-by-step reasoning:

The passage discusses what to do when it suddenly starts raining during a summer afternoon. It emphasizes that a car is the safest place to be during a storm, especially compared to tents or picnic shelters. It explicitly says that tents and shelters "offer little or no protection against lightning." It also notes that "lightning kills an average of 62 people in the US each year," highlighting its danger. Since camping usually involves tents and outdoor shelters, and the passage warns that these do not protect well from lightning, it implies that lightning is a danger specifically for people in those situations — including campers. Therefore, the statement "Lightning is a danger for campers" is: True

Answer: True

---

### Input

Passage: 
(1) The students in America usually begin high school at the age of 13 or 14.
(2) Some leave at the age of 16.
(3) But most finish the 4-year high school study.
(4) They do not get their first full-time work or begin college studies until they are 17 or 18 years old.
(5) The high school day is about 7 hours long.
(6) Part of the day is made into classes of about 50 minutes long.
(7) The students have lunch in the middle of the day.
(8) American students study English, history, math, science, art and languages.
(9) Some also learn job skills.
(10) They can learn how to use a computer or how to mend a car.
(11) Other students may learn by working in an office, a hospital or other places one day a week.
(12) Jean Wilton Anderson is 16 years old.
(13) She lives with her parents and two younger brothers in Bethesda, Maryland, near Washington D.C. Jean studies at Walt Whitman High School.
(14) There are about 1,500 students at the school.
(15) Most of them will go up to colleges.
(16) Jean wakes up early every morning.
(17) She begins school at about 7:30.
(18) This is her third year of high school.
(19) Every day she takes classes to learn English, world history, physics and trigonometry .
(20) She also has a class about different religions .
(21) And she plays the violin in music class.
(22) School ends at about 2 o'clock in the afternoon.
(23) Yet, Jean stays 2 or more hours longer every day for sports.
(24) Jean arrives home at about 5 o'clock in the afternoon.
(25) She eats dinner.
(26) Then she starts her homework.
(27) Students in America have their way of talking.
(28) They use the word "like" all the time.
(29) Jean and her girlfriends wear blue jeans and shirts or sweaters every day.
(30) The boys at her school also wear blue jeans.
(31) But they like to wear blue jeans that are several sizes too large for them.
(32) Like students of her age in most parts of the country, Jean begins to drive a car.
(33) She does not have her car.
(34) She must use the family's car.
(35) Most of the students in high school have their own cars.
(36) Many of them drive their cars to school every day.  

Statement: Students in high schools use the word "like" very often

Step-by-step reasoning:
responses:
To determine whether the statement "Students in high schools use the word 'like' very often" is supported by the passage, let's analyze the relevant parts of the passage step-by-step:

1. The passage provides a detailed description of the high school experience in America, including the subjects studied, the daily schedule, and extracurricular activities.

2. In sentence (27), the passage states, "Students in America have their way of talking."

3. Sentence (28) follows up by saying, "They use the word 'like' all the time."

4. The statement in question is about the frequency of the word "like" being used by high school students.

5. The passage explicitly states that students "use the word 'like' all the time," which directly supports the statement that students in high schools use the word "like" very often.

Based on this analysis, the statement is directly supported by the information provided in the passage.

Answer: True

(1) NOT all math skills are learned in the classroom.

(8) The scientists asked 64 14-year-olds to look at images   of yellow and blue dots that flashed on a computer screen for a fraction   of a second.
(9) Each image contained between 10 and 32 dots that varied in size.
(10) Some images contained twice as many blue dots  as yellow dots.
(11) In other images, however, the number of blue and yellow dots was nearly equal.
(12) For each image, the students were asked to estimate which color1 had more dots.
(13) The scientists found a wide variation in how well students could pick the color1 with the most dots.
(14) The scientists then looked at the students' math scores dating back to kindergarten.
(15) Children that performed best in the image test also scored the highest in standard math tests.
(16) The same finding held true at the other end of the spectrum  .
(17) Students who didn't score well on the image test received lower math scores.

The study found a correlation between students' performance on this test and their math scores from kindergarten onwards.

Then, at the end, answer with either "True" if the statement is supported, or "Not True" if it is not supported or contradicted.

Think step-by-step.
After your reasoning, return your answer in the following format:

```
Answer: Not True
```

---

### Example

Passage:
(1) It's a warm summer afternoon.
(2) Suddenly, a few raindrops fall on your arms, and before you know it, the sky is opened up.
(3) Heading to your car is almost always the safest choice.
(4) Your tent and a picnic shelter will keep you dry, but they offer little or no protection against lightning.
(5) Your car, on the other hand, will keep you dry and protect you from lightning strikes.
(6) Protecting yourself against possible lightning strikes is very important; lightning kills an average of 62 people in the US each year.
(7) If you keep all of the windows and doors closed in your car, you'll be able to return to your campsite safely.
(8) What else should you do?

Statement: Lightning is a danger for campers.

Step-by-step reasoning:

The passage discusses what to do when it suddenly starts raining during a summer afternoon. It emphasizes that a car is the safest place to be during a storm, especially compared to tents or picnic shelters. It explicitly says that tents and shelters "offer little or no protection against lightning." It also notes that "lightning kills an average of 62 people in the US each year," highlighting its danger. Since camping usually involves tents and outdoor shelters, and the passage warns that these do not protect well from lightning, it implies that lightning is a danger specifically for people in those situations — including campers. Therefore, the statement "Lightning is a danger for campers" is: True

Answer: True

Passage:

(2) She is nine years old and HIV positive .

(4) "That's when I found out who really was my friend, and who wasn't, "she said.
(5) "Quite a few people stopped visiting me and asked their children not to play with Simona any more.
(6) Because of this, quarrels began in my family with each trouble brought about by her diagnosis.
(7) My husband became more and more unfriendly toward me and toward Simona.
(8) He didn't understand how important it was for Simona to receive regular examinations by a doctor or why she needed proper treatment for her sickness."
(9) When her mother tried to enroll  Simona in a school, the teacher warned her that when other parents found out about her illness she would be shunned by the other children.
(10) Simona's mother looked for help and found a social worker from the Community Resource Center.
(11) The social worker helped her enroll Simona in another school and provided emotional, financial, and material support for the family.
(12) Simona's parents were able to ease the tension in the family.
(13) Her mother also now attends parenting classes and support groups at the Center.
(14) The Community Resource Center provides not only many needed resources, but also a place where women can gain skills and self-confidence.
(15) It provides children with a place where they are accepted.
(16) At the Center parents and children find a place where they can grow and find the support they need to stay together and care for each other.  

Statement: Simona was not accepted immediately by the first school.

Step-by-step reasoning:
responses:
1. The passage provides information about Simona, a nine-year-old girl living in Bucharest, Romania, who is HIV positive.

2. It describes the challenges Simona and her family faced after her diagnosis, including social isolation and family tensions.

3. Specifically, when Simona's mother tried to enroll her in a school, the teacher warned her that Simona would be shunned by other children once her illness was known. This implies that there was a concern about acceptance due to her condition.

4. The passage does not explicitly state that Simona was not accepted by the first school, but it does indicate that there was a warning about potential social rejection.

5. The passage then mentions that Simona's mother sought help from a social worker, who assisted in enrolling Simona in another school, suggesting that the first school was not a viable option for Simona due to the anticipated lack of acceptance.

6. The sequence of events implies that Simona was not accepted immediately by the first school, as her mother had to find an alternative solution with the help of a social worker.

Based on this reasoning, the statement "Simona was not accepted immediately by the first school" is supported by the passage.

Answer: True

### Example

Passage:
(1) It's a warm summer afternoon.
(2) Suddenly, a few raindrops fall on your arms, and before you know it, the sky is opened up.
(3) Heading to your car is almost always the safest choice.
(4) Your tent and a picnic shelter will keep you dry, but they offer little or no protection against lightning.
(5) Your car, on the other hand, will keep you dry and protect you from lightning strikes.
(6) Protecting yourself against possible lightning strikes is very important; lightning kills an average of 62 people in the US each year.
(7) If you keep all of the windows and doors closed in your car, you'll be able to return to your campsite safely.
(8) What else should you do?

Statement: Lightning is a danger for campers.

Step-by-step reasoning:

The passage discusses what to do when it suddenly starts raining during a summer afternoon. It emphasizes that a car is the safest place to be during a storm, especially compared to tents or picnic shelters. It explicitly says that tents and shelters "offer little or no protection against lightning." It also notes that "lightning kills an average of 62 people in the US each year," highlighting its danger. Since camping usually involves tents and outdoor shelters, and the passage warns that these do not protect well from lightning, it implies that lightning is a danger specifically for people in those situations — including campers. Therefore, the statement "Lightning is a danger for campers" is: True

(1) Sloth may be seen as a sin, but some of history's most accomplished men were fond of lounging around.
(2) Leonardo da Vinci enjoyed napping.
(3) So did Albert Einstein and Winston Churchill.
(4) Richard Buckminster Fuller advocated taking 30-minute naps every six hours.
(5) He is reported to have abandoned the practice only because" his schedule conflicted with that of his business associates, who insisted on sleeping like other men."
(6) No one has yet proved a correlation between napping and artistic brilliance or professional success, but an intriguing study published this week in the Archives of Internal Medicine claims to find a link between daytime siestas and good health.
(7) A team of researchers led by Androniki Naska of the University of Athens Medical School and Dimitrios Trichopoulos of Harvard's School of Public Health followed over 23,000 Greek patients with no history of coronary disease, cancer or stroke, for an average of six years.
(8) Their conclusion: napping just might save your life.
(9) The study found that the group of adults who took siestas (defined as 30-minute naps) at least three times a week had a third fewer deaths from heart disease than an equivalent group who did not sleep at all during the day.
(10) The benefit was greater for men than for women.
(11) (Whether women benefited at all was hard to estimate as there were too few deaths among them during the course of the study.)
(12) It was also greater for working men than for those who had retired.
(13) However, a number of previous studies done in the Mediterranean and in parts of Central America (where siestas remain common) have come up with conflicting results, but Dr Naska and Dr Trichopoulos argue that those studies have often been flawed.
(14) The subjects in some, for example, had survived heart attacks and may therefore have benefited more from napping than healthy individuals do.
(15) Given that all of the subjects of this new study were Greek, could the much-celebrated Mediterranean diet deserve credit, rather than the siestas?
(16) The firm answer from Dr Trichopoulos is" No" .
(17) And he is in a good position to say so, for it was he who did the pioneering research that put olive oil and a plant-based diet on the scientific map in the first place.
(18) Unlike some other siesta studies, his was controlled for diet, smoking, exercise and other relevant variables.
(19) The earlier findings about the benefits of the Mediterranean diet are confirmed, he says, and napping seems to help on top of that.
(20) Before buying a sofa for the office, however, it might be wise to consider the possibility of selection bias.
(21) Dr Trichopoulos concedes that" Type A"  personalities, whose hard-working lives may make them prone to heart attacks, are also much less likely to take naps during the day.
(22) That bias might be skewing the study's results.
(23) Even so, he advises, " Take a nap if you can."

### Example

Passage:

(2) Suddenly, a few raindrops fall on your arms, and before you know it, the sky is opened up.

(5) Your car, on the other hand, will keep you dry and protect you from lightning strikes.
(6) Protecting yourself against possible lightning strikes is very important; lightning kills an average of 62 people in the US each year.
(7) If you keep all of the windows and doors closed in your car, you'll be able to return to your campsite safely.
(8) What else should you do?

Statement: Lightning is a danger for campers.

Step-by-step reasoning:

The passage discusses what to do when it suddenly starts raining during a summer afternoon. It explicitly says that tents and shelters "offer little or no protection against lightning." It also notes that "lightning kills an average of 62 people in the US each year," highlighting its danger. Since camping usually involves tents and outdoor shelters, and the passage warns that these do not protect well from lightning, it implies that lightning is a danger specifically for people in those situations — including campers. Therefore, the statement "Lightning is a danger for campers" is: True

(4) For $ 1 a day, I worked eight hours straight, with no food breaks.

(6) Because the plantation owners were always watching us, I had to be on time every day and work as hard as I could.

(13) Nothing made me prouder than bringing home money to help my mother, father, two brothers and three sisters.
(14) This gave me self-esteem, one of the most important things a person can have.

(22) Then I hammered an empty tin can into the shape of a ball.
(23) And finally I dug two small holes in the ground and hit the ball back and forth.
(24) I practiced with the same devotion and intensity.
(25) I learned working in the field - except now I was driving golf balls with club, not oxen with a broomstick.

(1) A strong rupee is giving policymakers a new sort of headache
(2) THE Indian finance ministry's mid-year review, released this week, sees the external sector as a silver lining around the country's huge fiscal deficit. "

(5) They could be disappointed.
(6) For most of the past decade, the nominal value of the rupee has been allowed to decline gently against the dollar, by about 5% a year, thus staying fairly steady in real terms.
(7) This year, however, it has been appreciating in real terms (and, since June, nominally as well).
(8) It would have done so more sharply had the central bank not been buying dollars with gusto.
(9) Exporters of manufactured goods, obsessed with price competition from China, are aghast at the rise--and at the prospect held out by some forecasters that a sustained boom in India's IT exports means it will continue.
(10) The rupee's recent strength is only partly related to India's prowess in software and the mushrooming of "business-process outsourcing" in such projects as call-centres.
(11) The chunky surplus on invisibles owes more to remittances: non-resident Indians, attracted by the stability of the rupee and its higher interest rates, have been moving their offshore deposits back home.
(12) Similarly, Indian companies are borrowing more in dollars without selling rupees forward to hedge repayments.
(13) The trade deficit, meanwhile, has been shrinking, as imports grow slowly.
(14) The inflows have boosted foreign-exchange reserves by some $20 billion this year, to $66 billion, or 12 months'-worth of imports.

(16) In recent months, some controls have duly been eased.

(18) And non-resident Indians have been allowed to take out money acquired through inheritance, or from rents and dividends.
(19) Some commentators have taken all this as a harbinger of full capital-account convertibility.
(20) That is not on the cards.
(21) The experience of 1991, when India ran out of money, has left the central bank prone to caution--an approach it felt was vindicated by the East Asian crisis of 1997-98.
(22) With war in Iraq looming and a turbulent oil market, some risk aversion is understandable.
(23) India's fiscal deficit--some 10% of GDP and widening--is another reason for moving slowly.
(24) Just as one rating agency, Moody's, is considering upgrading India's external debt, another, Fitch, has warned that its local-currency rating is under threat.
(25) Nor is it certain that opening the capital account would mean a weaker rupee.
(26) It might even attract more capital inflows.
(27) As India's exporters are learning, convertibility is a two-way street.

Statement: India now can tackle adverse events in the foreign exchange market better

Step-by-step reasoning:
responses:
To determine whether the statement "India now can tackle adverse events in the foreign exchange market better" is supported by the passage, let's analyze the information provided:

1. **External Sector as a Silver Lining**: The passage begins by mentioning that the Indian finance ministry views the external sector positively, describing it as a "silver lining" around the fiscal deficit. This suggests some strength or advantage in the external sector.

2. **Current-Account Surplus**: The passage notes three consecutive quarters of current-account surplus, which is a positive indicator for the external sector and suggests a stronger position in the foreign exchange market.

3. **Foreign-Exchange Reserves**: The passage mentions swelling foreign-exchange reserves, which have increased by $20 billion to $66 billion, equivalent to 12 months' worth of imports. This substantial reserve acts as a buffer against adverse events in the foreign exchange market.

4. **Rupee Stability and Strength**: The rupee has been appreciating, and the central bank has been actively managing its value by buying dollars. This indicates a proactive approach to maintaining currency stability.

5. **Liberalization of Foreign-Exchange Controls**: Some foreign-exchange controls have been eased, making it simpler for individuals and non-resident Indians to manage foreign currency. This suggests a move towards a more flexible and resilient foreign exchange regime.

6. **Caution from Past Experiences**: The passage mentions the cautious approach of the central bank due to past experiences, such as the 1991 financial crisis and the East Asian crisis of 1997-98. This caution implies a preparedness to handle potential adverse events.

7. **Fiscal Deficit Concerns**: Despite the positive indicators, the passage also highlights concerns about India's fiscal deficit, which could impact its ability to handle adverse events.

8. **Capital Account Convertibility**: The passage notes that full capital-account convertibility is not planned, indicating a cautious approach to liberalization, which could be seen as a measure to prevent vulnerability to adverse events.

Overall, the passage provides several indicators that suggest India is in a better position to tackle adverse events in the foreign exchange market, such as increased foreign-exchange reserves, a current-account surplus, and a cautious approach to liberalization. These factors collectively support the statement that India can now better handle adverse events in the foreign exchange market.

Answer: True

responses:

2. Sentence 16 states that some controls have been eased in recent months, indicating that while some restrictions have been relaxed, not all have been removed.

### Example

(5) Your car, on the other hand, will keep you dry and protect you from lightning strikes.
(6) Protecting yourself against possible lightning strikes is very important; lightning kills an average of 62 people in the US each year.

(8) What else should you do?

Statement: Lightning is a danger for campers.

Since camping usually involves tents and outdoor shelters, and the passage warns that these do not protect well from lightning, it implies that lightning is a danger specifically for people in those situations — including campers. Therefore, the statement "Lightning is a danger for campers" is: True

### Example

Passage:

(3) Heading to your car is almost always the safest choice.
(4) Your tent and a picnic shelter will keep you dry, but they offer little or no protection against lightning.
(5) Your car, on the other hand, will keep you dry and protect you from lightning strikes.
(6) Protecting yourself against possible lightning strikes is very important; lightning kills an average of 62 people in the US each year.
(7) If you keep all of the windows and doors closed in your car, you'll be able to return to your campsite safely.
(8) What else should you do?

Statement: Lightning is a danger for campers.

Step-by-step reasoning:

The passage discusses what to do when it suddenly starts raining during a summer afternoon. It emphasizes that a car is the safest place to be during a storm, especially compared to tents or picnic shelters. It explicitly says that tents and shelters "offer little or no protection against lightning." It also notes that "lightning kills an average of 62 people in the US each year," highlighting its danger. Since camping usually involves tents and outdoor shelters, and the passage warns that these do not protect well from lightning, it implies that lightning is a danger specifically for people in those situations — including campers. Therefore, the statement "Lightning is a danger for campers" is: True

(7) But a foreigner might not have the knowledge and sympathy necessary to write the king's biography-not for a readership from within the kingdom, at any rate.
(8) There is no ideal position for such a task.
(9) The biographer has to work with the position he or she has in the world, adjusting that position as necessary to deal with the subject.
(10) Every position has strengths and weaknesses: to thrive, a writer must try to become aware of these, evaluate them in terms of the subject, and select a position accordingly.
(11) When their subjects are heroes or famous figures, biographies often reveal a democratic motive: they attempt to show that their subjects are only human, no better than anyone else.
(12) Other biographies are meant to change us, to invite us to become better than we are.
(13) The biographies of Jesus found in the Bible are in this class.
(14) Biographers may claim that their account is the "authentic" one.
(15) In advancing this claim, they are helped if the biography is "authorized" by the subject, this presumably allows the biographer special access to private information.
(16) "Unauthorized" biographies also have their appeal, however, since they can suggest an independence of mind in the biographer.
(17) In book promotions, the "unauthorized" characterisation usually suggests the prospect of juicy gossip that the subject had hoped to suppress.
(18) A subject might have several biographies, even several "authentic" ones.
(19) We sense intuitively that no one is in a position to tell the story of a life, perhaps not even the subject, and this has been proved by the history of biography.  

Statement: An authentic biography seldom appeals to its readers.

Step-by-step reasoning:
responses:
To determine whether the statement "An authentic biography seldom appeals to its readers" is supported by the passage, let's analyze the relevant parts of the passage step-by-step:

1. The passage discusses the challenges biographers face in maintaining objectivity and sympathy towards their subjects. It highlights the difficulty of finding the ideal position to write a biography, as every position has its strengths and weaknesses.

2. The passage mentions that biographies of heroes or famous figures often aim to show that their subjects are human, which can appeal to readers by making the subjects relatable.

3. It also states that some biographies are meant to inspire readers to become better, using the example of the biographies of Jesus in the Bible. This suggests that such biographies can have a strong appeal to readers who are looking for inspiration.

4. The passage discusses the concept of "authentic" biographies, which are claimed to be the true account of the subject's life. It notes that an "authorized" biography, which is considered authentic, might have special access to private information, potentially making it more appealing to readers interested in an in-depth and accurate portrayal.

5. Conversely, "unauthorized" biographies are noted for their appeal due to the independence of the biographer and the potential for revealing suppressed information, which might attract readers looking for gossip or alternative perspectives.

6. The passage does not explicitly state that authentic biographies seldom appeal to readers. Instead, it suggests that both authorized (authentic) and unauthorized biographies have their own appeals, depending on what the readers are looking for.

Based on this analysis, the passage does not support the statement that "An authentic biography seldom appeals to its readers." The passage indicates that authentic biographies can appeal to readers, especially those interested in accurate and authorized accounts.

Answer: Not True

(5) Your car, on the other hand, will keep you dry and protect you from lightning strikes.
(6) Protecting yourself against possible lightning strikes is very important; lightning kills an average of 62 people in the US each year.
(7) If you keep all of the windows and doors closed in your car, you'll be able to return to your campsite safely.
(8) What else should you do?

Statement: Lightning is a danger for campers.

The passage discusses what to do when it suddenly starts raining during a summer afternoon. It explicitly says that tents and shelters "offer little or no protection against lightning." It also notes that "lightning kills an average of 62 people in the US each year," highlighting its danger. Since camping usually involves tents and outdoor shelters, and the passage warns that these do not protect well from lightning, it implies that lightning is a danger specifically for people in those situations — including campers. Therefore, the statement "Lightning is a danger for campers" is: True

(1) Governments around the world are struggling to cope with the rising cost of health care, and of drugs in particular.
(2) Many rich countries have resorted to price controls, and some on the American left advocate them noisily.
(3) But drug firms maintain that America, where they are free to price patented pills largely as they please, is the engine of global pharmaceutical innovation, while price-controlling Europeans are free riders.
(4) That, says PhRMA, the industry's lobby based in Washington, D.C., is because price regulations seen in other rich countries" chill innovation, impede patients' access to the newest cutting-edge medicines, and trigger innovators to relocate to countries with more progressive public policy."
(5) A new study, written by Donald Light, a visiting professor at Stanford University, claims that European drug firms are more innovative than American ones, in spite of price controls.
(6) That flies in the face of an influential paper published in the same journal in 2006, which examined the geographic origins of drugs registered between 1982 and 2003 and concluded that favourable public policies had helped propel America to the top of the list.
(7) PhRMA has rushed to denounce Mr Light's study, insisting it gives a" distorted picture"  and understates the impact of" home-grown innovation" .
(8) The tiff exposes two fallacies that come from looking at a globalised industry through national spectacles.
(9) Both sides attribute new drugs to a specific country of origin (based on the location of the headquarters of the firm that first launches them).
(10) But Patricia Danzon of the Wharton Business School argues that this makes little sense when most big drug firms have laboratories in several countries and often acquire drugs under development from biotechnology firms located elsewhere.
(11) By the same token, firms develop drugs for the global market, observes Alan Garber of Stanford, not just for the local one.
(12) So the imposition of price caps in a given country will not necessarily quash innovation there, thanks to the lure of exports.
(13) The exception to this rule, of course, is the United States, which alone accounts for over 40% of global sales.
(14) So what would happen if America's Congress imposes price regulations?
(15) If the cuts are swinging, then the prize for inventors everywhere will be reduced.
(16) But short of such a radical scenario, pricing reform would not kill off innovation, according to Sanford Bernstein, a financial-research firm.
(17) It has analysed the likely impact of a cut of 20% in the prices Medicare, the government health scheme for the elderly, pays for its drugs.
(18) It concluded that the earnings per share of big pharmaceutical firms would drop by 3-8%.
(19) There is even reason to think that pricing reforms may boost innovation.
(20) Britain and Germany are pioneering comparative reviews of drugs' effectiveness and cost-benefit analyses aimed at reimbursing firms for new drugs based on how well they perform.
(21) Janssen-Cilag persuaded Britain's health service to accept Velcade, its expensive cancer drug, by offering a money-back guarantee if it did not work as well as promised.
(22) The American pharmaceutical lobby is violently opposed to making such an approach compulsory.
(23) Some suspect that its opposition stems from the fear that many expensive and profitable pills would be found to be of dubious value.
(24) But as Ms Danzon points out, " Comparative-effectiveness reviews are an indirect form of price control-but one that is consistent with encouraging innovation."

responses:

(2) The Pentagon will be relieved that the tribunals have started to show results after five years of controversy over the status of" enemy combatants" , claims of torture, the admissibility of forced confessions and a Supreme Court ruling last year that halted an earlier version of the tribunals.
(3) Yet the Hicks case is hardly an impressive start for America's offshore justice.
(4) Critics say the 31-year-old Mr Hicks, a former kangaroo skinner and alleged al-Qaeda fighter, faces a" kangaroo court" .
(5) Two of his civilian lawyers were barred from the hearing on March 26th.
(6) His military lawyer, Major Michael Mori, has been threatened with prosecution for harshly criticising the tribunals.
(7) The prosecutor, Colonel Morris Davis, said the defence lawyer may have breached military law that bans officers from using" contemptuous words"  against the president or senior officials.
(8) Major Mori filed a counter-charge against the prosecution, saying it was trying to intimidate him.
(9) In the end the motion will not be heard because Mr Hicks, looking pale and bedraggled, admitted the charge of" providing material support for terrorism" .
(10) A charge of" attempted murder in violation of the laws of war"  was dropped.
(11) As The Economist went to press, the tribunal was due to hear details of his plea and pass sentence, which Mr Hicks is expected to serve out in Australia.
(12) His father, Terry Hicks, said his son had had" five years of absolute hell"  since being captured in Afghanistan and allegedly suffering beatings, rape and forced injections in American custody-accusations rejected by the Pentagon.
(13) The guilty plea was just" a way to get home" , said Mr Hicks's father.
(14) Many in Australia regard Mr Hicks as more of a lost soul than a dangerous terrorist.
(15) Indeed, his charge sheet portrays him as little more than an al-Qaeda foot-soldier, and a poor one at that.
(16) His jihadi CV is pitiful compared with the evidence being given by some of the 14" high value detainees"  belatedly brought to Guantánamo from CIA secret prisons in September.
(17) They include al-Qaeda's operational chief, Khalid Sheikh Mohammed, who in a closed hearing to determine whether he was an" enemy combatant"  earlier this month boasted that he had organised the September 11th attacks" from A to Z" , and 30 other plots.
(18) Meanwhile, Ahmed Ghalfan Ghailani and Waleed bin Atttash, from Tanzania and Yemen respectively, have admitted supplying equipment for the bombing of the American embassies in Kenya and Tanzania in 1998.
(19) Many of these big names will face trial, and perhaps the death penalty.
(20) But the tribunals are expected to try only 60-80 of the more than 380 prisoners currently in Guantánamo.
(21) Over the years hundreds of detainees have been sent back to their countries, where some have been jailed and most have been released-and sometimes re-arrested.
(22) The Bush Administration says it wants to phase out and eventually close the jail.
(23) But it is unlikely to do so for some years, either because it lacks the evidence to prosecute detainees (even under the more lax military rules it is using), or because other countries are unwilling to take them back.  

Statement: The whole passage is permeated by an ironic tone towards the saying that the Hicks case showcases America's justice.

Step-by-step reasoning:
responses:
1. **Introduction of the Tribunals**: The passage begins by describing the start of the American military tribunals at Guantánamo Bay, specifically mentioning the case of David Hicks. It notes that the Pentagon is relieved that the tribunals are showing results after years of controversy.

2. **Criticism of the Tribunals**: The passage quickly shifts to a critical perspective, stating that the Hicks case is "hardly an impressive start" for America's justice system. It mentions critics who describe the tribunal as a "kangaroo court," suggesting a lack of legitimacy or fairness.

3. **Issues with Legal Representation**: The passage highlights issues with Hicks's legal representation, noting that two of his civilian lawyers were barred from the hearing and that his military lawyer faced threats of prosecution for criticizing the tribunals. This suggests a lack of fair legal process.

4. **Accusations of Intimidation**: The passage describes a counter-charge filed by Hicks's military lawyer, accusing the prosecution of intimidation. This further implies problems with the fairness and integrity of the tribunal process.

5. **Hicks's Guilty Plea**: The passage notes that Hicks pleaded guilty, but his father claims this was just a way to return home after enduring "five years of absolute hell." This suggests that the plea may not reflect genuine guilt but rather a desire to escape harsh conditions.

6. **Perception of Hicks**: The passage describes Hicks as more of a "lost soul" than a dangerous terrorist, with a "pitiful" jihadi CV compared to other detainees. This portrayal undermines the notion that his case is a significant showcase of justice.

7. **Comparison with Other Detainees**: The passage contrasts Hicks with other high-profile detainees who have admitted to serious crimes. This comparison suggests that Hicks's case is not representative of the broader justice being served at Guantánamo.

8. **Limited Scope of Tribunals**: The passage notes that only a fraction of the detainees will face trial, and many have been released or re-arrested. This indicates that the tribunals are not comprehensive or fully effective in delivering justice.

9. **Future of Guantánamo**: The passage concludes by mentioning the Bush Administration's intention to eventually close the jail, but notes obstacles to doing so. This suggests ongoing issues with the justice system at Guantánamo.

Overall, the passage presents a critical view of the tribunals and the Hicks case, highlighting issues with fairness, representation, and the broader justice system. The tone is indeed ironic towards the idea that the Hicks case showcases America's justice, as it emphasizes flaws and criticisms rather than successes.

Answer: True

### Example

Passage:
(1) It's a warm summer afternoon.
(2) Suddenly, a few raindrops fall on your arms, and before you know it, the sky is opened up.
(3) Heading to your car is almost always the safest choice.
(4) Your tent and a picnic shelter will keep you dry, but they offer little or no protection against lightning.
(5) Your car, on the other hand, will keep you dry and protect you from lightning strikes.
(6) Protecting yourself against possible lightning strikes is very important; lightning kills an average of 62 people in the US each year.
(7) If you keep all of the windows and doors closed in your car, you'll be able to return to your campsite safely.
(8) What else should you do?

Statement: Lightning is a danger for campers.

Step-by-step reasoning:

The passage discusses what to do when it suddenly starts raining during a summer afternoon. It emphasizes that a car is the safest place to be during a storm, especially compared to tents or picnic shelters. It explicitly says that tents and shelters "offer little or no protection against lightning." It also notes that "lightning kills an average of 62 people in the US each year," highlighting its danger. Since camping usually involves tents and outdoor shelters, and the passage warns that these do not protect well from lightning, it implies that lightning is a danger specifically for people in those situations — including campers. Therefore, the statement "Lightning is a danger for campers" is: True

Since camping usually involves tents and outdoor shelters, and the passage warns that these do not protect well from lightning, it implies that lightning is a danger specifically for people in those situations — including campers. Therefore, the statement "Lightning is a danger for campers" is: True

Think step-by-step.
After your reasoning, return your answer in the following format:

```
Answer: Not True
```

---

### Example

Passage:
(1) It's a warm summer afternoon.
(2) Suddenly, a few raindrops fall on your arms, and before you know it, the sky is opened up.
(3) Heading to your car is almost always the safest choice.
(4) Your tent and a picnic shelter will keep you dry, but they offer little or no protection against lightning.
(5) Your car, on the other hand, will keep you dry and protect you from lightning strikes.
(6) Protecting yourself against possible lightning strikes is very important; lightning kills an average of 62 people in the US each year.
(7) If you keep all of the windows and doors closed in your car, you'll be able to return to your campsite safely.
(8) What else should you do?

Statement: Lightning is a danger for campers.

Step-by-step reasoning:

The passage discusses what to do when it suddenly starts raining during a summer afternoon. It emphasizes that a car is the safest place to be during a storm, especially compared to tents or picnic shelters. It explicitly says that tents and shelters "offer little or no protection against lightning." It also notes that "lightning kills an average of 62 people in the US each year," highlighting its danger. Since camping usually involves tents and outdoor shelters, and the passage warns that these do not protect well from lightning, it implies that lightning is a danger specifically for people in those situations — including campers. Therefore, the statement "Lightning is a danger for campers" is: True

(1) Life really should be one long journey of joy for children who are born with a world of wealth at their tiny feet.

(4) Figures show that it is the rich who most often do away with themselves.
(5) Dr. Robert Coles, an internationally famous doctor, is the world's top expert on the influence of money on children.
(6) He has written a well-received book on the subject, The Privileged Ones, and his research shows that too much money in the family can cause as many problems as too little.
(7) Obviously there are certain advantages to being rich," says the 53-year-old doctor, such as better health, education and future work expectation.
(8) But most important is the quality of family life.
(9) Money can't buy love."
(10) It can buy a lot of other things, though, and that's where the trouble starts.
(11) Rich kids have so much to choose from that they often become confused.
(12) Their parents'over favoring can make them spoiled.
(13) They tend to travel more than other children, from home to home and country to country, which often makes them feel restless.
(14) "But privileged children do have a better sense of their positions in the world," adds Mr. Coles, "and they are more self-assu red."
(15) Today's rich parents perhaps have realized that their riches can be more of a burden than a favor to their children.
(16) So their priority is to ensure that their families are as rich in love as they are in money.  

Statement: Money can"t buy everything.

Step-by-step reasoning:
responses:
To determine whether the statement "Money can't buy everything" is supported by the passage, let's analyze the passage step-by-step:

1. **Introduction to Wealth and Happiness**: The passage begins by suggesting that children born into wealth should ideally have joyful lives. However, it quickly introduces the idea that wealth might not lead to happiness, as indicated by the mention of suicide statistics among the rich (sentences 1-4).

2. **Expert Opinion**: Dr. Robert Coles, an expert on the influence of money on children, is introduced. He has researched and written about the problems associated with too much money in families (sentences 5-6).

3. **Advantages and Limitations of Wealth**: Dr. Coles acknowledges certain advantages of being rich, such as better health, education, and future work expectations (sentence 7). However, he emphasizes that the most important factor is the quality of family life, implying that wealth alone is insufficient for happiness (sentence 8).

4. **Limitations of Money**: The passage explicitly states, "Money can't buy love" (sentence 9). This directly supports the idea that there are important aspects of life, such as love, that money cannot purchase.

5. **Problems Associated with Wealth**: The passage discusses various issues that can arise from wealth, such as confusion from too many choices, being spoiled, and restlessness from frequent travel (sentences 10-13).

6. **Self-awareness and Parental Realization**: While privileged children may have a better sense of their position in the world, the passage notes that today's rich parents are aware that wealth can be a burden. They prioritize ensuring their families are rich in love as well as money (sentences 14-16).

The statement "Money can't buy everything" is supported by the passage, particularly by the explicit statement "Money can't buy love" and the overall theme that wealth does not guarantee happiness or fulfillment in important areas of life, such as family love and emotional well-being.

Answer: True

You are given a passage and a statement. Your task is to determine whether the statement is supported by the passage. 

First, explain your reasoning step by step based only on the information in the passage.

Think step-by-step.

```
Answer: Not True
```

---

### Example

Passage:
(1) It's a warm summer afternoon.
(2) Suddenly, a few raindrops fall on your arms, and before you know it, the sky is opened up.
(3) Heading to your car is almost always the safest choice.
(4) Your tent and a picnic shelter will keep you dry, but they offer little or no protection against lightning.
(5) Your car, on the other hand, will keep you dry and protect you from lightning strikes.
(6) Protecting yourself against possible lightning strikes is very important; lightning kills an average of 62 people in the US each year.
(7) If you keep all of the windows and doors closed in your car, you'll be able to return to your campsite safely.
(8) What else should you do?

Statement: Lightning is a danger for campers.

Step-by-step reasoning:

The passage discusses what to do when it suddenly starts raining during a summer afternoon. It emphasizes that a car is the safest place to be during a storm, especially compared to tents or picnic shelters. It explicitly says that tents and shelters "offer little or no protection against lightning." It also notes that "lightning kills an average of 62 people in the US each year," highlighting its danger. Since camping usually involves tents and outdoor shelters, and the passage warns that these do not protect well from lightning, it implies that lightning is a danger specifically for people in those situations — including campers. Therefore, the statement "Lightning is a danger for campers" is: True

Answer: True

You are given a passage and a statement. Your task is to determine whether the statement is supported by the passage. 

First, explain your reasoning step by step based only on the information in the passage. 
Then, at the end, answer with either "True" if the statement is supported, or "Not True" if it is not supported or contradicted.

Think step-by-step.
After your reasoning, return your answer in the following format:

```
Answer: Not True
```

---

### Example

Passage:
(1) It's a warm summer afternoon.
(2) Suddenly, a few raindrops fall on your arms, and before you know it, the sky is opened up.
(3) Heading to your car is almost always the safest choice.
(4) Your tent and a picnic shelter will keep you dry, but they offer little or no protection against lightning.
(5) Your car, on the other hand, will keep you dry and protect you from lightning strikes.
(6) Protecting yourself against possible lightning strikes is very important; lightning kills an average of 62 people in the US each year.
(7) If you keep all of the windows and doors closed in your car, you'll be able to return to your campsite safely.
(8) What else should you do?

Statement: Lightning is a danger for campers.

Step-by-step reasoning:

The passage discusses what to do when it suddenly starts raining during a summer afternoon. It emphasizes that a car is the safest place to be during a storm, especially compared to tents or picnic shelters. It explicitly says that tents and shelters "offer little or no protection against lightning." It also notes that "lightning kills an average of 62 people in the US each year," highlighting its danger. Since camping usually involves tents and outdoor shelters, and the passage warns that these do not protect well from lightning, it implies that lightning is a danger specifically for people in those situations — including campers. Therefore, the statement "Lightning is a danger for campers" is: True

Answer: True
